coredata.dc:title: Indirect Z-scheme nitrogen-doped carbon dot decorated Bi2MoO6/g-C3N4 photocatalyst for enhanced visible-light-driven degradation of ciprofloxacin
coredata.dc:creator: Dang, Van Dien
Adorna, Joemer
Annadurai, Thamilselvan
Bui, Thi Ai Ngoc
Tran, Hai Linh
Lin, Liang-Yi
Doong, Ruey-An
coredata.dc:description: A novel nitrogen-doped carbon dot (NCD) decorated onto bismuth molybdate (Bi2MoO6) and carbon nitride (g-C3N4) (NCD@BMCN) nanocomposite was successfully synthesized for the photodegradation of ciprofloxacin (CIP) under the visible light irradiation. The homogeneous deposition of 10–20 nm NCD onto the surface of BMCN increases the specific surface area and photocurrent density, which can facilitate the good charge transfer efficiency for the enhanced photodegradation of CIP. The NCD@BMCN exhibits excellent visible-light-responsive photocatalytic activity over CIP degradation and the pseudo-first-order rate constants are in the range of 0.036–0.13 min−1, which are dependent on initial pH, photocatalyst dosage and existence of anions. The indirect Z-scheme mechanism for CIP photodegradation with hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and superoxide radicals (•O2 −) as the dominant reactive species is also proposed, in which NCD plays a role as the mediator to transfer electrons from the conduction band of Bi2MoO6 to the valence band of g-C3N4. Moreover, the NCD@BMCN photocatalyst exhibits good stability and reusability after five consecutive cycles of photodegradation. Results have demonstrated that the NCD@BMCN is a promising Z-scheme photocatalyst for the degradation of CIP, which can be further explored to synthesize a rapid, productive and low-cost NCD@BMCN nanocomposite for the treatment of pharmaceutical-contaminated water and wastewater.

The proposed pathways of CIP photodegradation over NCD@BMCN under visible-light irradiation.

(a) XRD patterns and (b) N2 adsorption–desorption curves, (c) TGA curve (inset: DTG curves), and (d) FTIR spectra of photocatalysts.

head.ce:title: Indirect Z-scheme nitrogen-doped carbon dot decorated Bi2MoO6/g-C3N4 photocatalyst for enhanced visible-light-driven degradation of ciprofloxacin

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel nitrogen-doped carbon dot (NCD) decorated onto bismuth molybdate (Bi2MoO6) and carbon nitride (g-C3N4) (NCD@BMCN) nanocomposite was successfully synthesized for the photodegradation of ciprofloxacin (CIP) under the visible light irradiation. The homogeneous deposition of 10–20 nm NCD onto the surface of BMCN increases the specific surface area and photocurrent density, which can facilitate the good charge transfer efficiency for the enhanced photodegradation of CIP. The NCD@BMCN exhibits excellent visible-light-responsive photocatalytic activity over CIP degradation and the pseudo-first-order rate constants are in the range of 0.036–0.13 min−1, which are dependent on initial pH, photocatalyst dosage and existence of anions. The indirect Z-scheme mechanism for CIP photodegradation with hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and superoxide radicals (•O2 −) as the dominant reactive species is also proposed, in which NCD plays a role as the mediator to transfer electrons from the conduction band of Bi2MoO6 to the valence band of g-C3N4. Moreover, the NCD@BMCN photocatalyst exhibits good stability and reusability after five consecutive cycles of photodegradation. Results have demonstrated that the NCD@BMCN is a promising Z-scheme photocatalyst for the degradation of CIP, which can be further explored to synthesize a rapid, productive and low-cost NCD@BMCN nanocomposite for the treatment of pharmaceutical-contaminated water and wastewater.

ce:section.ce:para: Pharmaceuticals, which contain a wide variety of medications, have been used to protect human and veterinary health. However, the release and disposal of unused pharmaceuticals to the environment have deteriorated water quality because of the increased antibiotic concentration in aquatic environments. Ciprofloxacin (CIP), a distinctive second-generated fluoroquinolone antibiotic, is commonly utilized in human and veterinary medication in view of its extensive action against microscopic organisms. The concentration of CIP in surface water has been greatly accumulated because of its extensive and excessive usage, and then subsequently increases the widespread antibiotic resistance and potential threats to aquatic biota and human well-being [1]. Because the traditionally physicochemical and biological treatment processes cannot remove CIP effectively due to its stable chemical structure, developing a highly effective technique to decompose CIP from water is urgently essential.

Photocatalysis has been reported as one of the promising techniques to eliminate recalcitrant chemicals in water environments [2,3]. Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4, CN) is a well-known metal-free semiconductor, which can directly absorb visible light because of its suitable bandgap (2.7 eV). Besides, the tri-s-triazine ring arrangement makes CN extraordinarily stable in a wide range of temperatures and various chemicals [4]. CN has been reported to be viable for CO2 reduction [5], biosensing [6], hydrogen evolution [7,8], and organic pollutant degradation applications [9–11]. However, the speedy hole-electron (h+-e−) recombination rate and low visible-light fascination ability restrict the practical applications of unblemished CN. Therefore, a significant effort including co-catalysts [12], single-atom catalysis [13], crystallinity engineering [14], and combination with different semiconductors [15,16] has been carried out to enhance the photoactivity of CN toward pollutant degradation. It is well-accepted that Z-scheme photocatalysis is an effectively alternative method to enhance the photocatalytic performance of heterojunctions [17,18]. Under visible-light irradiation, the photogenerated electron-hole pairs can be produced from both heterojunction photocatalysts. An electron in the lower conduction band (CB) of the first photocatalyst can combine with a hole in the higher valence band (VB) of the second photocatalyst to form one pair at the interface, resulting in the production of more oxygen-containing free radicals like ●OH and ●O2 −. Hence, the whole Z-scheme system can not only generate electron-hole pairs with high redox potentials, but can also promote the spatial separation of electrons and holes to accelerate the photodegradation efficiency and rate [18,19].

Bismuth molybdate (Bi2MoO6) is one of the promising candidates in photocatalysis because of its layered aurivillius-related oxide structure and visible-light absorption capability. The crystal structure of Bi2MoO6 comprises the stack of [Bi2O2]2+ interceding among MoO4 2− plates, which possesses an excellent visible-light-driven property [20–22]. Recently, several studies have reported that the combination of Bi2MoO6 and g-C3N4 (BMCN) can create a good heterostructured photocatalyst [23,24]. The photo-excited electrons from the conduction band (CB) of Bi2MoO6 may transfer to the valence band (VB) of g-C3N4 rapidly, resulting in the generation of plenty of electrons in CB of g-C3N4 and holes in VB of Bi2MoO6. By this combination, therefore, more reactive radicals will be produced for photocatalytic activity. However, the use of BMCN for the photodegradation of pharmaceutical pollutants has received less attention, and the low light-harvesting and electron transfer efficiencies hamper the photocatalytic efficiency of BMCN. Therefore, the preparation of a new type of visible-light-driven BMCN composite with enhanced photoactivity for feasible applications is necessary.

Nitrogen-doped carbon dot (NCD) is a zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial, which has been used as an excellent component in the fabrication of photocatalysts because of its outstanding properties such as upconversion fluorescence behavior and good photoinduced electron transfer properties [25,26]. It has been reported that the combination of NCD with semiconductors can broaden the absorption spectrum to the visible light region as well as reduce the recombination rate of electron-hole pairs to improve the photodegradation performance by effective separation of holes and electrons [27,28]. It is believed that the NCD can serve as the mediator to transfer electrons from Bi2MoO6 to CN [26,27], which means that the decoration of NCD onto BMCN can generate the Z-scheme photocatalyst for the enhanced photocatalytic degradation of pollutants. However, the fabrication of NCD@BMCN for the Z-scheme photodegradation of CIP has not been reported yet.

Herein, a visible-light-response NCD@BMCN photocatalyst has been constructed by the hydrothermal method and then used for the photodegradation of CIP under different environmental conditions. As shown in Scheme 1 , urea was selected as the raw material for the preparation of CN, which serves as the support and photocatalyst for photocatalysis. Next, Bi2MoO6 (BM) was deposited onto the CN surface hydrothermally at 160℃ for 4 h to form BMCN nanocomposite. Final, NCD prepared from citric acid and ethylenediamine was decorated onto the surface of BMCN to form the visible-light-driven NCD@BMCN nanocomposites. The NCD@BMCN with large surface area, low impedance, and high photocurrent density can produce more h+-e− pairs to enhance the photoactivity of NCD@BMCN over CIP degradation under different environmental conditions including pH, dosage, and inorganic anions. The NCD@BMCN can maintain photostability and can be recycled at least 5 times without the loss of photoactivity and mineralization efficiency. In addition, the indirect Z-scheme mechanism for CIP degradation is proposed based on radical production and Mott-Schottky experiments. The photodegradation pathways of CIP were also described according to the LC-MS results.

Urea (CO(NH2)2) and citric acid (C6H8O7) were procured from Riedel-de Haën/JK Baker. Ethylenediamine (EDA, C2H4(NH2)2) was obtained from J. T. Baker. Bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O) and sodium molybdenum oxide dihydrate (Na2MoO4·2H2O) were obtained from Alfa Aesar. Diethylene glycol (C4H10O3) was bought from Merck. CIP (C17H18FN3O3) was supplied by Fluka. Ammonium oxalate monohydrate (C2H8N2O4) was purchased from Acros Organics. Tert-butanol (C4H10O) and 1,4-benzoquinone (C6H4O2) were provided by Sigma Aldrich. All the other chemicals and reagents were of analytical grade and utilized as received from suppliers unless otherwise stated. Deionized water (DI water, 18.2 MΩ cm) was used to prepare all of the solutions in this study.

The NCD was prepared using a hydrothermal route from our previously reported method with minor adjustments [29]. Shortly, 1 mmol of citric acid was mixed with 3 mmol of EDA in a glass beaker containing 5 mL of DI water. Next, the solution was transferred into an autoclave tube (Teflon®-lined, 316 stainless-steel) and put into an oven at 160℃ for 4 h. After cooling down, the NCD solution was obtained with yellowish color, then dialyzed in a 1 kDa dialysis bag for 24 h to remove the unreacted chemical agents. The NCD solution was kept in a closed container at 4℃ for further use.

The CN was prepared from pyrolysis and ultrasonication using urea as the raw material. Notably, 30 g urea in a crucible was heated to 500℃ at 5℃ min−1 for 2 h, and then adjusted to 550℃ at 10℃ min−1 for 2 h. After cooling to room temperature, the pale-yellow powder was pulverized to obtain bulk g-C3N4. The bulk C3N4 was exfoliated by ultrasonication in ethanol for 1 h to obtain g-C3N4 nanosheet (CN). The mixture was then filtered and dried to obtain CN powder.

The BMCN was synthesized through a hydrothermal route. Briefly, 2 mmol Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was mixed with 1 mmol Na2MoO4·2H2O in 20 mL of diethylene glycol by vigorous stirring for 30 min. 1 mmol CN dispersed in 30 mL of ethanol was then added into the beaker under stirring until the formation of a homogeneous mixture. The mixture was ultrasonicated for 1 h, then directly transferred to autoclave tubes for hydrothermal treatment at 160℃ for 4 h. The obtained yellowish BMCN precipitate was washed 3 times with ethanol and then stored at 4℃.

The NCD@BMCN was fabricated by adding the as-prepared NCD solution to the BMCN suspension followed by stirring for 24 h. The obtained product was precipitated and washed 3 times to remove unattached NCD, and then the composite was filtered and dried overnight using a vacuum oven at 80℃.

The size and shape of NCD@BMCN were determined by a cold field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, Hitachi SU8010). JEOL JEM-ARM200F transmission electron microscope (TEM) and JEM-2100F TEM at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV were employed to identify the size and morphology of photocatalysts. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured by a Bruker D8 X-ray diffractometer (XRD) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å) at 40 mA and 40 kV was used to identify the crystallinity of NCD@BMCN. The specific surface area and pore texture of photocatalysts were determined by a Micromeritics ASAP 2020 porosimetry system at 77 K. The thermal property of NCD@BMCN was determined using Mettler Toledo thermo-gravimetric analyzer (TGA) in a temperature range of 30–800℃ at 10℃ min−1. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Bruker Vertex 80v) was utilized to acquire FTIR spectra in the wavenumber range of 500–4000 cm−1. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was used to investigate the chemical composition of elements in photocatalysts with Thermo Fisher Escalab Xi + photoelectron spectrometer using Al Kα radiation at 1486.6 eV. The X-ray absorption near-edge structure (XANES) measurement for the C and O K-edge was conducted with beamline BL20A located at the National Synchrotron Radiation Research Center (Hsinchu, Taiwan). The electron storage ring and beam current are adjusted at 1.5 GeV and 300 mA, respectively.

Optical characterization of nanomaterials was expressed by UV–visiblespectra with Hitachi U-4100 UV–visible. Time-resolved FL decay spectra were measured by a confocal optical analysis system (Bruker Biocatalyst) at 420–1000 nm with a Picoquant 405 nm CW/Pulse diode laser. The electrochemical performance of photocatalysts, including electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), photocurrent, and Mott-Schottky plots were carried out on Autolab PGSTAT302N electrochemical analyzer (Metrohm B.V, Switzerland). A three-electrode system with Pt wire and Ag/AgCl electrode as the counter and reference electrodes, respectively, was used to conduct the experiment. The working electrode was prepared using the mixture of NCD@BMCN, PVDF, and conductive carbon black at a weight ratio of 8 : 1 : 1, and then dispersed in N-methyl pyrrolidone (NMP) solvent followed by sonication for 30 min to ensure a uniform solution. The mixture was then coated on FTO glass with 1 × 1 cm2 area at the mass loading of 0.1 mg cm−2 for electrochemical measurements. Moreover, 0.1 M sodium sulfate was prepared as an electrolyte solution. A 300 W Xenon lamp was used as a light source for photocurrent measurement. EIS experiment was conducted in a frequency range of 100 kHz to 0.01 Hz. The Mott-Schottky plots were recorded in a potential range of −1.0–0.01 V (versus Ag/AgCl) at a constant frequency of 1000 Hz.

The photocatalytic degradation experiments were performed in an 8-tube photoreaction system (Rayonet RPR-100) supplied with 8 × 8 W visible lamps under ambient conditions. The spectrum of visible-light lamp follows the Gaussian distribution in the wavelength range of 380–580 nm with the major peaks of 404, 436 and 547 nm (Fig. S1, Supplementary data). The photodegradation of 8 tubes of samples was conducted under the irradiation intensity of 77 mW cm−2. In each experiment, 25 mg of the as-prepared NCD@BMCN was added into a quartz tube containing 25 mL of DI water followed by ultrasonication for 10 min. Appropriate volumes of 50 mg L−1 CIP stock solution was added into the mixture to get the final CIP concentrations of 1–10 mg L−1. The pH in the reactor was controlled using 0.1 M HCl or 0.1 M NaOH to the desired value of 5–10.

The effect of anions on the photodegradation was examined by the addition of 0.1 M difference anions including HCO3 −, Cl−, H2PO4 −, and NO3 − to the reactor. Moreover, the influence of organic content on CIP photodegradation was evaluated by adding 1–20 mg-C L−1 humic acid to the solution. Before the photodegradation, solutions were stirred vigorously for 30 min in the dark to achieve the adsorption equilibrium. After the adsorption, the visible light source was turned on to start the photodegradation. Although the irradiation with light during the photodegradation may increase the temperature, the change in temperature can be neglected after 30 min of irradiation because the intensity of visible light used in this study is relatively low.

During the photocatalytic process, 1 mL of suspension was withdrawn within specific intervals and allowed to pass through a 0.45 µM polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) membrane. The residual concentration of CIP was determined by a high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC, Waters 616 series) equipped with a C-18 column (GL Sciences Inertsil ODS-2) and a Waters 2487 absorbance detector at 278 nm. A mixture of acetonitrile and phosphate buffer at a ratio of 13:87 (v/v) was prepared as the mobile phase, which was pumped through at a flow rate of 1 mL min−1. All the photocatalytic experiments were performed in triplicate for precision and accuracy. Moreover, the photodegradation of CIP over NCD@BMCN was carried out in lake water from Cheng Kung Lake, National Tsing Hua University (Hsinchu, Taiwan) and operated for 5 cycles to evaluate the applicability of the developed photocatalyst. For comparison, the photodegradation in lake water was conducted with the optimal condition identical to the DI water.

The total organic carbon (TOC) analyzer (TOC-L, Shimadzu) was used to analyze the mineralization extent of CIP. The isoelectric point (IEP) of the photocatalyst was examined by using a Zetasizer Nano ZS system (Malvern Analytical) at pHs 5–10. The photodegradation intermediates of CIP were determined by a high-performance liquid chromatograph-tandem mass spectrometer (HPLC-MS system). The mass spectrum was recorded by directly injecting 2 μL of each sample into an Impact HD Q-TOF mass spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) with an electrospray ionization (ESI) source in the m/z range of 50–1500. The radical scavengers including 0.1 M ammonium oxalate monohydrate (AO), 1,4 benzoquinone (1,4-BQ) and tert-butanol (t-BuOH) were used to trap holes (h+), superoxide anion radicals (•O2 –) and hydroxyl radicals (•OH), respectively [30]. Moreover, the photogenerated radicals were examined by using EPR spectrometer (Bruker, ELEXSYS-II E580 FT/CW system) working at an X-band range of 9.49–9.88 GHz and 8.02 mW power. 5 mM 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) was used as the spin-trapping agent where DMPO–•OH and DMPO–•O2 – adducts were formed in aqueous and methanol solutions, respectively.

The SEM and TEM images were used to characterize the morphology and nanostructure of the as-prepared photocatalysts. Fig. 1 a illustrates a layered structure of CN with large and numerous stacking nanosheets. The TEM image shown in Fig. 1b clearly indicates that CN is a thin 2-D nanosheet. It is interesting that Bi2MoO6 exhibits a rod-like shape with a length of 100–150 nm, which is homogeneously dispersed onto CN surface (Fig. 1c). The TEM image of BMCN displays that the diameter of Bi2MoO6 nanorods is in the range of 5–10 nm (Fig. 1d). The cross-linking of the CN nanosheets with Bi2MoO6 nanorods results in a rough surface and thus provides more active sites for CIP to enhance the photodegradation efficiency and rate.

The TEM image of NCD@BMCN in Fig. 1e signifies that the lattice configuration of CN with d-spacing of 0.344 nm is relevant to the (002) lattice plane of g-C3N4. In addition, the rod-shaped Bi2MoO6 positioned on the CN nanosheets surface displays the interplanar spacing of 0.25 nm, which is correlated to the (131) plane of Bi2MoO6. The fast Fourier transform (FFT) image derived from the HRTEM image (inset of Fig. 1e) confirms the presence of NCD with hexagonal diffraction planes of sp2 graphitic carbon in the inner region [29], elaborating that NCD particles, Bi2MoO6 rods, and CN nanosheets are well-integrated to form a heterojunction. The HRTEM image of NCD@BMCN in Fig. 1f clearly indicates that the diameter of NCD is 15 nm, and the clear lattice fringe of the as-prepared NCD with an inter-planar spacing of 0.24 nm (inset of Fig. 1f) is in good agreement with the (100) facet of graphitic carbon. Moreover, the EDX measurement elucidates the compositions inside NCD@BMCN. As shown in Fig. S2 (Supplementary data), the elemental mapping shows that the percentages of C, N, O, Mo, and Bi are 31.4, 5.2, 28.8, 9.4, and 25.2%, respectively. It is noteworthy that the C/N is 6.04, which is far higher than the theoretical atomic C/N ratio of g-C3N4 (0.75). This is mainly attributed to the introduction of NCD, which contain mainly carbon elements and small amount of N dopant to the BMCN nanocomposites, and, therefore, increases the percentage of C element obviously.

Fig. 2 a shows the XRD patterns of CN, BMCN and NCD@BMCN. The graphitic structure of CN is defined by two broad peaks centering at 13.1° and 27.4° 2θ, which are the (100) and (002) interlayer diffraction of conjugated aromatic of g-C3N4 with the interplanar spacing of 0.344 nm (JCPDS 87-1526) [31]. For BMCN, four well-defined peaks at 28.0°, 32.4°, 46.6°, 55.3°, and 58.6° 2θ are observed, which can be assigned as the (131), (002), (062), (331) and (191) planes of Bi2MoO6 (JCPDS 84-0787) [32]. The peak of CN at 27.4° 2θ is not clearly observed on BMCN, presumably due to the fact that the diffraction peaks of (131) plane of Bi2MoO6 (2θ = 28.0°) and (002) plane of CN are close. Therefore, the highly dispersive heterojunction structure of BMCN results in the indistinguishable peaks between Bi2MoO6 and CN. For NCD@BMCN, a low-intensity diffraction peak centered at 23.6° 2θ confirms the successful formation of NCDs [33]. Besides, the peaks at 28.5° and 39.8° 2θ are the (002) and (100) planes of graphitic carbon, respectively [29,34]. It is interesting to note that XRD peaks in NCD@BMCN shift to the high angle region in comparison with the pure BMCN, which is mainly attributed to the fact that the doping of NCDs onto the composites enlarges the lattice parameters of the crystal [35].

It is well-known that large specific surface area can provide more active sites for photodegradation, and, therefore, the specific surface area and pore texture of three photocatalysts were determined. As shown in Fig. 2b, the isotherms show typical Type IV isotherms with H3 hysteresis loops in the P/Po range of 0.5–0.95, elaborating the mixture of meso-macro porous structure originated from the stacking holes between the lamellar materials [36]. In addition, the pore size distribution exhibits bimodal curves centered at 2.3 and 70.0 nm (Fig. S3, Supplementary data), confirming the coexistence of meso- and macro-pores [37]. The addition of Bi2MoO6 to CN slightly reduces the SBET and overall pore volume of the heterostructure to 29.9 m2 g−1 and 0.185 cm3 g−1, respectively, which is in good agreement with the reported data [38]. The decrease in SBET is probably due to the occupation of pores of CN by Bi2MoO6 nanorods [39]. The incorporation of NCD in the BMCN framework increases its SBET to 43.7 m2 g−1. In addition, the pore volume also increases to 0.22 cm3 g−1, which can increase the active sites of NCD@BMCN to accommodate the adsorption of CIP.

Thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) was used to determine the thermal stability of compounds. The TGA curves of NCD@BMCN based nanomaterials and corresponding DTG curves are displayed in Fig. S4a (Supplementary data). It is shown that all of the materials undergo a two-stage decrease in weight. The first step is attributed to the weight loss in the vaporization of interstitial H2O molecules at a temperature below 200℃, which accounts for 16%, 5%, and 13% weight loss for CN, BMCN, and NCD@BMCN, respectively. The second weight-loss stage of CN starts from 400℃ and is completed at 700℃, which can be supported by the corresponding DTG peak of CN (inset of Fig. S4a) [24]. The decrease in weight is mainly attributed to the oxidation of carbon in CN. The addition of Bi2MoO6 to CN significantly increases the thermal stability, which starts to lose weight from 450℃ and 75% of its original weight is retained at 800℃, which means that the weight loss of 20 wt% is the added amount of CN. The incorporation of NCD to BMCN prompts a slight decrease in the thermal stability of the heterostructure as the DTG curve of NCD@BMCN plateaus at –0.1% °C−1. In addition, 62% of NCD@BMCN remains upon heating to 800℃, which is attributed to the fact that NCD is also thermally degraded along with g-C3N4, and the added amount of NCD is around 13% based on the difference in weight loss between BMCN and NCD@BMCN.

FTIR was employed to understand the surface functionality and hydrophilicity of the photocatalysts. As shown in Fig. S4b (Supplementary data), all three photocatalysts exhibit a broad peak at 3000–3400 cm−1, corresponding to the hydroxyl (–OH) groups from surface adsorbed water molecules and carboxylic groups in the materials [29]. A series of specific peaks at 1250–1650 cm−1 are contributed from the aromatic C–N heterocycles in CN and the doped N onto the benzene ring in NCD [29]. The FTIR peaks at 1650–1470 cm−1 are typically associated with the aromatic C–N stretching caused by the heptazine ring undulations, while the peaks at 1250–1430 cm−1 correspond to the heptazine ring out-of-plane bending vibrations [40]. The observed peak at 807 cm−1 could be ascribed to the out-of-plane bending mode of the s-triazine ring [41]. The peak at 725 cm−1 for BMCN and NCD@BMCN is the asymmetric stretching mode of MoO6 by its equatorial oxygen atoms vibration. In addition, the peaks located at 835, 797, and 566 cm−1 belong to the Mo–O stretching vibration modes of the corner-sharing (MoO6)6– octahedron and the bending vibration of the MoO6 octahedron, which overlaps with the bending Bi–O bond of (Bi2O2)2+ in Bi2MoO6 [42].

The surface chemistry and compositional states of the as-prepared photocatalysts were determined by XPS measurement. The XPS survey spectra in Fig. 3 a shows the existence of C 1s, (284.1 eV), N 1 s (400.1 eV), and O 1s (532.0 eV) peaks for g-C3N4. The O 1s signal of BMCN and NCD@BMCN shifts to 529 eV, presumably due to the presence of Bi2MoO6. Also, the BMCN and NCD@BMCN show the core-level peaks for bismuth (Bi 4d and 4f) and molybdenum (Mo 3d), which are consistent with the EDX results of the as-prepared photocatalysts [42]. The absence of other elements in the survey spectrum confirms the high purity of the as-prepared materials, which is in consistence with the XRD results.

The deconvoluted peaks of the C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s core-level spectra of CN are displayed in Fig. 3b–d, respectively. The C 1s core-level region (Fig. 3b) shows four main peaks, i.e., C–N=C (288.2 eV) in C–N heterocycles, C–OH (286.9 eV), C=C in adventitious carbon (285.2 eV), and sp2 carbon (293.5 eV) [43]. The N 1s core-level region (Fig. 3c) exhibits C–N=C (398.8 eV) in sp2 hybridized nitrogen in triazine rings, amino functional groups in the form of N–H (400.8 eV), and π excitation (404.5 eV) [19]. Similarly, the O 1s (Fig. 3d) core-level peak mainly possesses two peaks coming from N–C–O (533.0 eV) and interstitial H2O (532.5 eV) from the adsorbed water on the surface of CN [43]. Moreover, the C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s of BMCN and NCD@BMCN are also investigated (Fig. 3e–g), and find that all the peak positions shift considerably due to the hybridization of NCD with BMCN. The C 1s spectra (Fig. 3e) in BMCN and NCD@BMCN show peaks of C–N=C and the sp2 C–C bond in s-triazine rings at 287.5 and 284.8 eV, respectively [38]. Two satellite peaks of C–OH (285.6 eV) and C–N (284.1 eV) corroborate the coordination between carbon and nitrogen atoms in the space lattice of CN [42,43]. Similarly, the N 1s peaks (Fig. 3f) show three main peaks at 397.8, 399.6, and 396.0 eV, which can be assigned as C–N=C, N–H, and C–N bonds, respectively. The wide asymmetric O 1s peak (Fig. 3g) can be deconvoluted into three different peaks. The peaks occurring at 529.0, 530.0 eV are metal–oxygen bonds of Bi-O and Mo-O, respectively, while the peak at 531.7 eV is the characteristic peak of the surface –OH bonding [38]. Fig. 3h shows the deconvoluted peaks of Bi 4f core-level spectra for NCD@BMCN. The distinct spectrum of Bi2MoO6 at 157.8 and 163.1 eV are typical Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 peaks, respectively [38,42,44,45]. A spin-energy separation of 5.3 eV confirms that bismuth is in the predominant Bi3+ oxidation state. The small satellite peaks at 163.9 and 158.5 eV correspond to the Bi–O bond of Bi2MoO6. Moreover, peaks at 231.0 and 234.2 eV, which are ascribed as the Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2, respectively, can be clearly observed in the Mo 3d spectrum (Fig. 3i), confirm the existence of Mo6+ in BMCN with an observed spin-energy separation of 3.2 eV [24]. The two small satellite peaks at 234.9 and 231.8 eV is the MoOx complex in the (MoO6)6- octahedra [46].

XANES spectroscopy was used to examine the electronic structure and the surface chemistry of NCD@BMCN based nanomaterials. The C K-edge spectra of NCD, CN, BMCN, and NCD@BMCN are shown in Fig. 4 a. For all the four samples, the highest feature of electron yield at 289.8 eV is the C 1s core exciton state due to the quantum confinement effect in NCD and CN [47]. In addition, the peak at 296.3 eV can be assigned as the excitation of C 1s to π* transition localized at C = O bonds of carboxylic acid and carbonyl moieties [48]. The peak at 299.7 eV can be attributed to the transition to π* states of C − O bonds. The 286.8 eV in NCD is the C 1s → π* transition of graphene. For BMCN and NCD@BMCN, the lowest energy peak at 285.5 eV is the transition of C 1s into π* symmetry [48]. The characteristic peak of 288.5 eV is mainly attributed to the C 1s → π* transition of C − O − C bond [49], which indicates the fingerprint of sp2-hybridized carbon (graphitic) structures of CN.

The O K-edge spectra of NCD, CN, BMCN, and NCD@BMCN are shown in Fig. 4b. The feature at 532.4 eV is the O 1s core level, while the broad peak in the region of 539–545 eV is attributed to the O 1s → σ* transitions of O–H. The peak at 530.8 eV is the O 1s → π* transition of C = O originated from the COOH groups [48], which are in good agreement with results obtained in XPS and FTIR. Besides, the peak at 535 eV for CN, BMCN, and NCD@BMCN confirms the existence of C–O group in the CN nanosheets.

Fig. 5 a shows the UV–visible spectra of NCD@BMCN based nanomaterials. The absorption edge of CN nanosheets is about 443 nm, which belongs to the n–ᴨ* transition. The incorporation of BM as a co-catalyst of CN results in the red-shift in the absorption edge to 520 nm in comparison with pure CN nanosheets, likely due to the fact that BM has a smaller bandgap than that of CN. It is noted that the red-shift in absorbance of a photocatalyst signifies its absorption capacity of visible light. When NCD is further incorporated into the BMCN composite, the visible-light-responsive region is further improved, which indicates that the NCD can synergistically extend the absorption of the visible-light range of BMCN. This result is further supported by the color change of the photocatalysts from yellow to dark brown (inset of Fig. 5a), resulting in the significant improvement of light absorption capacity.

The bandgap (Eg) of photocatalyst can be estimated by the absorption-edge wavelength. As illustrated in Fig. 5a, the absorption-edges of CN, BMCN and NCD@BMCN are measured to be 443, 470, and 599 nm, respectively. This corresponds to the Eg of 2.81, 2.64, and 2.07 eV for CN, BMCN, and NCD@BMCN, respectively, which is in good agreement with Tauc plot (Fig. S5, Supplementary data). The Eg of CN in this study is a little higher than the reported value of 2.7 eV [50], likely due to the quantum confinement effect when exfoliation of bulk-CN into nanosheets. It is also noted that the Eg of Bi2MoO6 is determined to be 2.65 eV (Fig. S6, Supplementary data). The combination of CN with Bi2MoO6 reduces the Eg to 2.64 eV, and then to 2.07 eV when conductive NCD is added, which means that the NCD@BMCN can generate e−-h+ pairs after receiving the visible light <600 nm.

The transient photocurrent response and EIS of NCD@BMCN based nanomaterials were further investigated. Fig. 5b displays the photocurrent responses of the three photocatalyst electrodes via off–on cycles under visible light irradiation. The photocurrent response of the NCD@BMCN heterojunction is considerably higher than those of CN and BMCN with CN being significantly lowest. In addition, the photocurrent of NCD@BMCN can be maintained at 3.1 µA after 6 cycles, which is 2 times higher than that of BMCN. Several plausible reasons can be accounted for the enhanced photocurrent. The incorporation of conductive NCDs to the binary Bi2MoO6/g-C3N4 heterostructure can reduce the electric resistance and impedance of BMCN, resulting in the acceleration of electron transfer rate as well as the improvement on electron-hole separation efficiency. In addition, the light absorption of NCD@BMCN shows a significant increase in comparison with pure CN and BMCN, especially in the visible light range of 420–800 nm (Fig. 5a). It is noted that the upconversion property of NCD, which has been reported previously [25,26], allows NCD@BMCN to absorb long wavelength visible light and then convert to short wavelength with high energy, which means that NCD@BMCN can receive more energy to generate e−-h+ pairs. As a result, more photons can be produced and transferred to BMCN, leading to a significant increase in photocurrent density. Moreover, the NCD serves as a photogenerated charge separation and migration agent to mediate the reaction between electrons from the CB of BM and holes in the VB of CN. This would reduce the recombination rate of e−-h+ pairs in NCD@BMCN, and subsequently enhances the photoelectrochemical performance to produce high photocurrent [24].

To provide a better insight regarding the complex charge transfer resistance and the separation efficiency at the photocatalyst-electrolyte interface, the EIS experiment was performed and the result is shown in Fig. 5c. It is recognized that the semi arc found in the high frequency range (105–103 Hz) represents the charge transfer resistance (Rct), which indicates the electron transfer between the electrode and the electrolyte. The arc radius of CN is almost indistinguishable, which is the characteristic of electric double-layer capacitor (EDLC) materials. Also, the EIS curve of CN exhibits good linearity in the low-frequency region. Moreover, the NCD@BMCN has a smaller arc radius than BMCN, which proves the effective electron-hole pair separation and rapid interfacial charge transfer in NCD@BMCN [51].

The time-resolved fluorescence (TRFL) decay spectra can further confirm the decrease in charge carrier recombination. As presented in Fig. 5d, the average lifetime for CN is 16.29 ns, which is longer than those of BMCN (9.37 ns) and NCD@BMCN (3.52 ns). This result indicates the existence of an additional non-radiative relaxation of the electron transfer from CN nanosheets to Bi2MoO6 and NCD. The short non-radiative relaxation of NCD@BMCN elaborates the significantly improved photoinduced electron transfer from CN to Bi2MoO6 and NCD. Moreover, the short lifetime of NCD@BMCN in comparison with CN and BMCN indicates the lower recombination rate of e−-h+ pairs, which may lead to the enhanced photocatalytic activity of NCD@BMCN.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photodegradation of CIP by different photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption of CIP onto the surface of photocatalysts was first tested prior to the performance of photocatalytic experiments. As shown in Fig. S7a (Supplementary data), 7.6–13% CIP is adsorbed by different photocatalysts after 30 min of reaction under dark conditions. Obviously, NCD@BMCN shows the highest adsorption efficiency compared to CN and BMCN because of its large SBET, which can provide more active sites for CIP molecules to migrate. When switching on the light source, CIP is rapidly photodegraded by NCD@BMCN based photocatalysts under visible light irradiation. It is noted that there is no obvious decrease in CIP concentration observed in the absence of photocatalysts under visible light (Fig. 6 a), indicating that the direct photolysis of CIP is negligible. CN shows the lowest degradation efficiency and only 20% of CIP is removed after 30 min of irradiation. The efficiency of CIP photodegradation increases to 65% in the presence of BMCN nanocomposite. Moreover, NCD@BMCN exhibits its excellent performance on visible-light-responsive photodegradation and 99% of CIP is removed within 30 min. The superior photodegradation activity of NCD@BMCN is attributed to the excellent hydrophilic property, large SBET, and light absorption capability, which can be supported by the surface characterization results of NCD@BMCN. Accordingly, the improved visible light absorption of NCD@BMCN can generate more electron-hole pairs, while the low impedance and good crystallinity of NCD@BMCN reduce the recombination rate of electron-hole pairs. In addition, the high SBET and oxygenated functional groups, which were identified by XPS and FTIR, make the surface of NCD@BMCN more hydrophilic and active to adsorb CIP readily. Through the enlarged lattice of NCD@BMCN nanocrystals shown in the XRD patterns, electron/hole and electrolyte ions can thus be transported easily in the large space to accelerate the photocatalytic degradation of CIP under visible light irradiation.

The pseudo-first-order rate equation is further used to quantify the photodegradation efficiency of CIP by different photocatalysts, and the linear relationship between ln(C/C0) and reaction time (t) is shown in Fig. S7b (Supplementary data). It is clear that the NCD@BMCN exhibits the highest photocatalytic activity in comparison with other photocatalysts. The pseudo-first-order rate constant (kobs) for CIP photodegradation by NCD@BMCN is 3.4 and 15 times higher than those of BMCN and CN, respectively (Fig. 6b), signifying that NCD plays an important role in boosting the photocatalytic activity. In addition, the NCD@BMCN displays the enhanced TOC removal efficiency of 87.2%, while only 6.8 and 24.5% of TOC removal are obtained for CN and BMCN, respectively after 30 min of irradiation.

Several studies have prepared CN based nanocomposites such as BiOBr/carbon dots/CN and Bi2MoO6/CN for the photocatalytic degradation of CIP [52–57]. As shown in Table S1 (Supplementary data), the kobs values are found to be competitive with that by NCD@BMCN obtained in this study. Moreover, the photodegradation of CIP by NCD@CN and NCD@BM was also investigated. As illustrated in Fig. S7c (Supplementary data), the degradation efficiencies of CIP by NCD@CN and NCD@BM are 54 and 68%, respectively, after 30 min of irradiation. The kobs values for CIP photodegradation by NCD@CN and NCD@BM are 0.04 and 0.028 min−1, respectively (Fig. S7d, Supplementary data), which is much lower than that by NCD@BMCN. These results clearly corroborate that the combination of NCD, BM and CN to form NCD@BMCN is a promising strategy for the preparation of photocatalyst to degrade CIP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on the photodegradation efficiency
ce:section.ce:para: pH is one of the most important factors to influence the photocatalytic activity of NCD@BMCN, and, therefore, the effect of pH on the photocatalytic degradation of CIP by NCD@BMCN was performed at pHs 5–10. As displayed in Fig. 6c, 63% and 73% of CIP are photodegraded at pHs 5 and 6, respectively, after 30 min of irradiation. The increase in pH improves the photodegradation efficiency of CIP, and 90–98% photodegradation efficiency of CIP by NCD@BMCN is observed at pH 7–8. However, the photodegradation efficiency of CIP decreases slightly to 90–95% when pH increases to 9–10, clearly indicating that pH 8 is the optimal pH value for the photodegradation of CIP by NCD@BMCN and is used for further experiments. Moreover, the adsorption of CIP onto NCD@BMCN as a function of pH was also examined in the dark. As illustrated in Fig. S8 (Supplementary data), the adsorption of CIP on NCD@BMCN is only 5–7% under the acidic condition of pH 5–6 after 30 min of reaction, presumably due to the repulsive force between the positively charged NCD@BMCN and cationic CIP adsorption repels each other, leading to the weak adsorption of CIP on NCD@BMCN surface. In addition, the adsorption efficiency of CIP increases to 9–13% at pH 7–8, and then decreases to 7–9% when further increases the pH to 9–10, which is consistent with the photodegradation patterns of CIP at different pHs.

The change in solution pH after the photodegradation is further measured and the result is shown in Table S2 (Supplementary data). It is clear that the solution pH changes slightly after the photodegradation when the initial pH values are both in the range of pH 5–7 and pH 9–10. However, a 1.0-unit decrease in pH is observed at the initial pH value of 8. The obvious decrease in pH value at the optimal pH value is mainly attributed to the reaction between hydroxide (OH−) and h+ to generate hydroxyl radicals (OH– + h+ → •OH), and subsequently produce more radicals to accelerate the photodegradation efficiency and rate of CIP.

The change in photodegradation efficiency is mainly attributed to the interaction between the pKa of CIP and the isoelectric point (pHIEP) of NCD@BMCN. It is known that pKa1 and pKa2 of CIP are 6.09 and 8.74, respectively, which means that CIP is positively charged (CIP+) at pH < 6.09 due to the protonation of amine groups, while zwitterionic CIP (CIP±) is obtained when pH is in the range of 6.09–8.74 because of the deprotonation of carboxylic groups. Moreover, the CIP species becomes anionic form (CIP-) when the pH is higher than 8.74. It is noteworthy that the isoelectric point (pHIEP) of NCD@BMCN is 6.4 (Fig. 6d), indicating that NCD@BMCN is positively charged at pH < 6.4, and then becomes negatively charged when pH > 6.4. This result also supports that the adsorption of CIP onto NCD@BMCN at pH 5–6 is low because of the repulsive force between the positively charged NCD@BMCN and cationic CIP. When the pH increases to 7–8, the photodegradation is accelerated because more deprotonated CIP is generated to react with reactive oxygen species (ROS) on the surface of photocatalyst. This is because the deprotonated form of CIP has higher electron negativity and is more susceptible to be oxidized than the protonated form. When the pH further increases to 9–10 which is higher than the pKa2 of CIP, however, the completely deprotonated CIP is hard to react with negatively charged NCD@BMCN with zeta potential < –30 mV, resulting in the decrease in the photocatalytic degradation rate of CIP at high pH values.

The photodegradation of CIP by NCD@BMCN at different pHs can be quantified by pseudo-first-order rate equation. As illustrated in Fig. 6d, the kobs for CIP photodegradation is in the range of 0.03–0.08 min−1 at pH 5–7, and then increases to 0.13 min−1 at pH 8. The kobs decreases obviously to 0.08–0.1 min−1 when further increases the pH to 9–10. It is interesting to note that the fraction of CIP species is pH-dependent, and the kobs for CIP photodegradation can thus be expressed as the function of species-specific rate constant (ki) and molar fraction of CIP species (αi):

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the initial concentration of CIP on the photocatalytic performance
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 7 a shows the degradation rate of NCD@BMCN at different initial concentrations of CIP ranging from 1 to 10 mg L−1. After 30 min of irradiation, the degradation efficiencies are 99.5, 99.0, and 98.0% when the initial concentrations of CIP are 1, 3, and 5 mg L−1, respectively. The efficiency slightly decreases to 91.5–88% when the CIP concentration increases to 7–10 mg L−1. Moreover, the kobs for CIP photodegradation decreases from 0.16 min−1 (r2 = 0.975) at 1 mg L−1 to 0.06 min−1 (r2 = 0.990) at 10 mg L−1 (inset of Fig. 7a). The decrease in rate constant at high concentration of CIP is mainly attributed to the fact that the photodegradation of CIP over NCD@BMCN is the surface-mediated reaction and the limited active sites of NCD@BMCN nanophotocatalyst hamper the rapid adsorption of CIP from bulk solution to the surface of NCD@BMCN. Usually, the Langmuir-Hinshelwood kinetics model (L-H model) can be employed to describe this phenomenon [58].

where ro is the initial rate for CIP photodegradation, CCIP is the CIP concentration; kr is the maximum initial rate, and KF is the Langmuir coefficient of CIP. Fig. 7b shows the change in initial rate for CIP photodegradation as a function of CIP concentration. The initial rate of CIP photodegradation increases from 0.148 mg L-1 min−1 at 1 mg L−1 CIP to 0.581 mg L−1 min−1 at 10 mg L−1 CIP. The simulated curve fit the experimental data quite well with a correlation coefficient (r2) of 0.992, which signifies that the photodegradation of CIP by NCD@BMCN follows the L-H model, and the maximum initial rate and Langmuir coefficient are 0.764 mg L−1 min−1 and 0.35 L mg−1, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the dosage of NCD@BMCN on the photocatalytic activity
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the optimal amount of NCD@BMCN photocatalyst for the degradation of CIP, various amounts of NCD@BMCN from 0.5 to 1.5 g L−1 were optimized at pH 8. As presented in Fig. 7c, the photodegradation efficiency of CIP increases gradually from 63.5% to 98% when the added amount of NCD@BMCN increases from 0.5 to 1.0 g L−1. Conversely, the efficiency descends to 87.5% when the concentration of photocatalyst increases from 1.0 to 1.5 g L−1. The kobs values shown in the inset of Fig. 7c are 0.04, 0.07, 0.12, 0.08, and 0.07 min−1 (r2 = 0.975–0.991) when the added amounts of NCD@BMCN are 0.5, 0.7, 1.0, 1.3, and 1.5 g L−1, respectively. The low degradation efficiency at low photocatalyst dosage is contributed from the limited active sites on the NCD@BMCN surface. Obviously, the increase in added amount of NCD@BMCN can not only increase the actives sites for CIP to adsorb but also receive more visible light to photogenerate more reactive radicals, resulting in the enhancement of photocatalytic performance. However, the addition of high concentrations of photocatalyst at 1.3–1.5 g L−1 would cause the masking effect on light scattering to restrict the penetration of light through the solution, leading to the decrease in photodegradation efficiency and rate. Therefore, the optimized dosage of 1.0 g L−1 is used for further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of inorganic anions and organic matters on the photocatalytic activity
ce:section.ce:para:
Several inorganic anions broadly exist in water and wastewater, which may have a significant impact on the CIP photodegradation efficiency and rate over photocatalyst. These anions may affect the adsorption of CIP on the surface of NCD@BMCN photocatalyst as well as serve as radical scavengers or promoters to change the degradation rate [59,60]. As illustrated in Fig. 7d, the addition of 0.1 mM anion inhibits the performance of NCD@BMCN over CIP photodegradation. The CIP degradation efficiency decreases from 99% in the absence of anions to 78–96% when 0.1 mM anions are added. This result can be attributed to the fact that inorganic anion species can act as the h+ and •OH radical scavengers [34]. In addition, the competition of inorganic anions with CIP for the active sites on the surface of NCD@BMCN photocatalyst is also a plausible reason [40]. As shown in the inset of Fig. 7d, the kobs decreases obviously from 0.12 min−1 in the absence of anions to 0.106, 0.085, 0.065, and 0.052 min−1 in the presence of NO3 −, H2PO4 −, Cl−, and HCO3 −, respectively. It has been reported that the presence of anions could serve as the scavengers for h+ and ●OH, consequently decreases the photodegradation efficiency [60]. As illustrated in Eqs. (6)–(9), anions including NO3 −, HCO3 −, Cl− and, H2PO4 − can react with ●OH radicals to form another radical with low oxidizing ability such as ●NO2 −, ●CO3 −, ●ClOH−, and ●HPO4 − radicals [61–64], and subsequently decrease the photodegradation efficiency and rate of CIP. Moreover, the movement of anions, which depends on the diameter and effective charge, plays an important role in affecting the photocatalytic activity of photocatalysts. In this case, the NO3 − ions have the lowest effective charge compared to the other anions, leading to less inhibition to photodegradation [65].
(6) NO3 − + ●OH → ●NO2 − + ½ O2 + OH−
(7) HCO3 − + ●OH → ●CO3 − + H2O
(8) Cl− + ●OH → ●ClOH− + H2O
(9) H2PO4 − + ●OH → ●HPO4 − + H2O

Dissolved organic constituents such as macromolecules and natural organic matters are commonly found in chemical oxygen demand matters in wastewater, which may influence the photocatalytic performance of NCD@BMCN. Fig. S9 (Supplementary data) shows the effect of various concentrations of humic acid (HA) on the photodegradation efficiency of CIP. The photodegradation efficiency of CIP is inversely proportional to the added concentration of HA. Specifically, the kobs decreases from 0.12 min−1 in the absence of HA to 0.05 min−1 in the presence of 20 mg-C L−1 of HA, mainly attributed to the competition for active sites of NCD@BMCN between HA and CIP. Moreover, HA can be a light attenuator to decrease the absorption of the photon with CIP as well as a scavenger for ROS generated from the photocatalysts, resulting in the decrease in photodegradation efficiency and rate of CIP.

Recyclability is an indicator to assess the stability of the NCD@BMCN photocatalyst. As depicted in Fig. 8 a, the photodegradation efficiency of CIP by NCD@BMCN can reach 99% after five cycles, confirming the photostability of NCD@BMCN. The kobs value shown in Fig. S10 (Supplementary data) decreases slightly from 0.13 min−1 initially to 0.11 min−1 after 5th cycle of reaction, clearly indicating the stability of NCD@BMCN over CIP photodegradation. The slight decrease in rate constant can be credited to the accumulation of byproducts on the photocatalyst surface from the previous cycles, which compete for the active sites of NCD@BMCN with CIP. The second reason is probably due to the loss during the recycling since the material needs to be separated and dried for the next cycle. In addition, the TOC removal efficiency is 80% for the 1st cycle and then slightly decreases to 77–79% during the 2nd to 5th cycles, which is in good agreement with the rate constants. Moreover, the XRD patterns of NCD@BMCN after 5 cycles of photodegradation still exhibit the clear peaks, which are identical to those in the as-prepared NCD@BMCN photocatalysts (Fig. S11, Supplementary data). This result signifies that NCD@BMCN is a promising and stable photocatalyst for practical photodegradation application.

The applicability of NCD@BMCN photocatalyst was examined using lake water as the matrix. The water quality of lake water is presented in Table S3 (Supplementary data). The TOC concentration of 22.4 mg-C L−1 with alkalinity of 130 mg-CaCO3 L−1 at pH 8.5 indicates that lake water contains relatively high dissolve organics with HCO3 − as the major alkalinity species. In addition, 5 mg L−1 CIP was spiked into the lake water containing 1 g L−1 NCD@BMCN for visible-light-driven photodegradation. The result shown in Fig. 8b signifies that the photodegradation activity of NCD@BMCN in the lake water is superior, and a nearly complete photodegradation of CIP with kobs of 0.093 min−1 is observed. Moreover, the NCD@BMCN can still be recycled at least 5 times in lake water, and the kobs for CIP photodegradation after 5 cycles is 0.082 min−1 (Fig. S10, Supplementary data). It is noteworthy that the reaction rate of CIP photodegradation by NCD@BMCN in lake water is lower than that in DI water, presumably attributed to the influence of COD matters and anions in lake water. However, the photocatalytic activity of NCD@BMCN still can maintain at least 90% of the initial activity, clearly elaborating the superiority of the developed photocatalyst in real environmental samples.

As shown in Fig. 9 a the removal efficiency of CIP decreases significantly when t-BuOH and 1,4-BQ are added into the solutions, signifying that hydroxyl and superoxide radicals are the leading species to dominate the photocatalytic activity. Besides, the photodegradation efficiency of CIP by NCD@BMCN in the presence of AO is similar to that in the absence of radical scavengers, clearly indicating the insignificant participation of hole in the degradation process of CIP. In addition, the kobs for CIP photodegradation are 0.1, 0.017, and 0.009 min−1 when AO, 1,4-BQ, and t-BuOH are added (Fig. 9b), corroborating that CIP is mainly degraded by the oxygen-derived radicals such as •OH and •O2 –, while hole (h+) (Eq. (10)) only has a little contribution to the photodegradation process. The hydroxyl radicals (•OH) can be generated from the reaction of OH– with h+ produced from the VB of Bi2MoO6 (Eq. (11)), while the •O2 – radicals can be produced from the reaction between O2 with electrons (Eq. (12)). In addition, some •OH radicals can be derived from the reactions of •O2 – (Eqs. (13) and (14)). Since •OH radicals are stronger oxidizing agent than that of •O2 – radicals, the •OH radical serves as the major oxygen-derived radical for photodegradation of CIP under visible light irradiation.
(10) NCD@BMCN + hυ → e– CB + h+ VB
(11) OH− + h+ VB → •OH
(12) O2 + e– CB → •O2 –
(13)
•O2 – + H2O → •OOH + OH
(14)
•OOH + H+ + e– → 2•OH

To further determine the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) by NCD@BMCN under visible light irradiation, As illustrated in Fig. 9c, four characterized peaks of DMPO–•OH species at the intensity of 1:2:2:1 are observed. The peak intensity can be maintained after 15 min of irradiation. When changing to methanol solution, the DMPO can trap •O2 – radicals, resulting in the formation of DMPO–•O2 – adducts (Fig. 9d), which are comparable with those from standard DMPO–•O2 – in Fig. S12 (Supplementary data). These EPR results are in good agreement with the radical scavenging experiments, confirming the production of •OH and •O2 – radicals by NCD@BMCN under visible light irradiation.

Fig. 10 illustrates the proposed Z-scheme mechanism of NCD@BMCN for CIP degradation under visible light. The CB and VB of CN and Bi2MoO6, determined by Mott-Schottky plot (Fig. S13, supplementary data), can be estimated to be −1.08 and 1.73 eV for CN, and −0.13 and 2.54 eV for Bi2MoO6, respectively. After being irradiated by visible light, NCD in the nanocomposite can boost the excitation by receiving visible light to produce electron-hole pairs. The CN and Bi2MoO6 are also excited to generate the photogenerated electrons and holes on their CBs and VBs, respectively. Moreover, NCD serves as the electron mediator to transfer electrons from CB of Bi2MoO6 to the VB of CN to combine with holes that existed there, leading to the reduction of e-h recombination in CN. As a result, an extremely high yield of electrons and holes are generated in the CB of CN and the VB of Bi2MoO6, respectively. Because the CB position (−1.08 eV) of CN is more negative than the O2/•O2 − redox potential (−0.33 eV) [66], the electrons can be reacted with the adsorbed O2 to form •O2 − radicals, and then to other ROS (•HO2, H2O2, and •OH). In addition, since the VB position (2.54 eV) of Bi2MoO6 is more positive than the redox potential (2.38 eV) of OH−/•OH pairs, •OH radicals can thus be generated from the reaction between the holes and H2O [3].

The determination of intermediates generated from the photodegradation process is a vital step to elucidate the degradation pathway. The mass spectra of intermediates produced from the CIP photodegradation process after 15 and 30 min of irradiation are displayed in Fig. S14 (Supplementary data). Based on the LC-MS result, the proposed degradation pathway of CIP by NCD@BMCN is illustrated in Scheme 2 . It is noticed that the initial transformation of CIP is a breakthrough point in photodegradation activity. In pathway 1, the piperazine ring of CIP is partially photodegraded to produce P1 (m/z 277) through piperizine breaking, followed by cutting the CH3–NH bond to obtain P2 (m/z 263) by demethylation. In the next step, cyclopropyl and amine groups are removed to achieve P3 (m/z 207) by cleavage. Later, fluorine, quinolone, carbonyl and carboxyl groups are eliminated to give P4 (m/z 123) by defluorination, carbonylation and decarboxylation. The •OH and •O2 − radicals can also attack CIP to cause rings cleavage, leading to the release of P5 (m/z 291) (pathway 2). The cleavage of the quinolone moiety in P5 could further generate P6 (m/z 213) by decarboxylation, and the cyclopropyl group is cut to form P7 (m/z 174). The last step of this pathway could be the elimination of methyl, carbonyl and amine groups to produce P8 (m/z 133). Moreover, the •OH radicals can attack CIP at C6 position, and then fluorine atom is substituted by −OH because this place accepts electron density through the resonance effect. The •OH radicals continue to attack the piperazine ring through piperazine breaking and defluorination to generate P9 (m/z 261) (pathway 3). The pyrrole ketone ring is further shattered by radicals and then hydroxylated to produce P10 (m/z 197) by decarboxylation. The carbon–nitrogen bond is further deaminated to become P11 (m/z 166) because of the intrinsic instability of the broken ring. Subsequently, −OH and C groups are cut down by ring breaking to form P12 (m/z 99). It is noted that the chemical structures of intermediates are still complicated and hard to calculate the oxidation numbers of carbon elements in the intermediate. However, a TOC removal efficiency of 80% is observed after the photodegradation, which means that the metabolites can be further degraded into CO2, H2O and other low molecular compounds.

In this study, the indirect Z-scheme NCD@BMCN photocatalyst has been successfully synthesized using facile strategies to degrade CIP with excellent performance. The 0-D NCD and 1-D Bi2MoO6 nanorods are well-dispersed on 2-D CN nanosheets, which effectively decreases the bandgap from 2.64 eV for BMCN to 2.07 eV for NCD@BMCN. The decoration of 10–20 nm NCD onto BMCN can not only increase the specific surface area to 43.7 m2 g−1 but can also enhance the photocurrent by a factor of 2. Moreover, NCD can serve as a light absorber as well as a charge carrier to transfer the photogenerated electrons from the CB of Bi2MoO6 to the VB of CN, resulting in the production of more electron-hole pairs to enhance the photocatalytic activity over CIP degradation. A nearly complete photodegradation of CIP by NCD@BMCN is achieved within 30 min of visible light irradiation and the photodegradation efficiency is high highly dependent on the environmental conditions including pH, photocatalysts dose, and anions. The photodegradation efficiency of CIP shows good performance at pH 8 when the dosage of photocatalyst is 1 g L−1. Moreover, the presence of foreign anions in the solution reduces the photocatalytic performance of NCD@BMCN. The scavenger experiments and EPR spectra clearly indicate that the •OH and •O2 – radicals are the main contributors to the photodegradation process. The NCD@BMCN photocatalyst shows the high stability when the efficiency do not change considerably after five consecutive recycles in lake water with spiked CIP. The indirect Z-scheme and possible reaction pathway are also proposed based to clarify the photocatalytic degradation of CIP by NCD@BMCN. The results of this study signify that NCD@BMCN is a promising photocatalyst with a great visible-light-responsive and electric conductive property, which can open an avenue to fabricate Z-scheme based nanocomposites for practical applications in environmental purification of pharmaceutical-contaminated water and wastewater.
